chunk_summary: This section discusses microbial causes of malabsorption, focusing on giardiasis and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), with condition-specific dietary and treatment strategies, including metronidazole for giardiasis and structured SIBO management based on risk factors and testing availability.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of microbial contributors like giardiasis and SIBO in malabsorption syndromes.
Learn condition-specific dietary modifications, including low-lactose approaches for lactose intolerance.
Familiarize with diagnostic tools such as stool ova-and-parasite examination and breath testing for SIBO.
Review evidence-based treatment regimens, including metronidazole for giardiasis and antibiotics for SIBO.
key_facts: Giardiasis is a common cause of malabsorption, treatable with metronidazole 5 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days.
Lactose intolerance in giardiasis can be managed with a low-lactose diet rather than milk dilution.
SIBO diagnosis can involve breath testing, with methane ≥10 ppm indicating intestinal methanogen overgrowth.
SIBO treatment in children includes 7–14 day courses of nonabsorbable or systemic antibiotics, only after addressing dietary and infection-related factors.
WHO guidelines emphasize continuing breastfeeding and age-appropriate feeding during treatment of microbial malabsorption.
reference_quote: A practical treatment option supported in the WHO pocket book is metronidazole 5 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days.
